request_to_amend_name_after_judgment.incorrect_names: "Maria E. Rodriquez" (misspelled last name) and "Maria Rodriquez" (missing middle initial) as they appear on the judgment entered on 2022-03-18.
request_to_amend_name_after_judgment.correct_legal_name_and_actually_used_nam: My correct legal name is "Maria Elena Rodriguez." I also regularly use the shortened name "Maria E. Rodriguez" on work documents, bills, and identification. The judgment should list me as "Maria Elena Rodriguez, also known as Maria E. Rodriguez."
request_to_amend_name_after_judgment.facts_supporting_this_request:
I am the defendant in this matter. I am requesting that the court amend the judgment to correct the spelling of my last name and to reflect my correct legal name, which is "Maria Elena Rodriguez." 

1. Nature of the error:
In the original judgment entered on 2022-03-18, my name is listed as "Maria E. Rodriquez" and in some portions of the case file as "Maria Rodriquez." The error is that my last name is spelled with a "g" before the "u" (Rodriguez), not with a "q" (Rodriquez). This appears to be a clerical or typographical error that occurred when the plaintiff first filed the complaint and was then carried forward into the judgment.

2. How I discovered the mistake:
I discovered the error when I attempted to set up a payment plan and later when I applied for a car loan in August 2023. The credit union loan officer, Mr. James Lee at Glendale Community Federal Credit Union, pulled my credit report and asked me about a judgment under the name "Maria E. Rodriquez." He questioned whether that judgment belonged to me because the spelling did not match the name on my driver’s license (which reads "Maria Elena Rodriguez"). I then obtained a copy of the judgment from the court and saw that my name was indeed misspelled.

3. Why the correction is necessary:
Because the judgment is under an incorrect spelling of my last name, it has caused confusion with creditors and potential lenders. Some automated systems are not properly associating the judgment with my existing credit file under "Rodriguez," and others are flagging it as a possible identity discrepancy. This has resulted in additional verification steps, delays in processing my car loan, and questions about whether I might be using different identities.

Additionally, I am in the process of renewing my state professional certification as a medical assistant. The background check vendor hired by my employer, Highland Park Family Clinic, initially could not locate the judgment under my correct name and then later flagged it as a potential mismatch. This has created stress and the need for me to repeatedly explain the clerical spelling error. Having the judgment reflect my correct legal name will help ensure accurate records with my employer, licensing agencies, and credit bureaus.

4. Evidence supporting my correct legal name:
To support this request, I can provide (and, if required, will file) the following documents showing my correct legal name as "Maria Elena Rodriguez":
   a. A certified copy of my California driver’s license issued by the California Department of Motor Vehicles, which lists my name as "RODRIGUEZ, MARIA E."
   b. A copy of my Social Security card, which shows my name as "Maria E Rodriguez."
   c. My birth certificate from Los Angeles County, issued in 1984, which lists my full name as "Maria Elena Rodriguez."
   d. Recent utility bills (Southern California Edison and Los Angeles Department of Water and Power) and bank statements from Chase Bank, all addressed to me at 1842 Cypress Avenue, Apt 3B, Los Angeles, CA 90065, under the name "Maria E. Rodriguez."

5. Consistency with prior filings and appearances:
Although the complaint and judgment list me as "Rodriquez," whenever I have personally signed any documents in connection with this case, I have signed as "Maria E. Rodriguez." For example, on my written response filed on 2021-06-02, my signature block reads "Maria E. Rodriguez" even though the caption above it uses the misspelled version. At the case management conference on 2021-09-14, when I checked in with the clerk, I verbally confirmed that my name is spelled R-O-D-R-I-G-U-E-Z, and I remember the clerk making a note of the correct spelling on a sheet, but it appears the correction was not carried forward into the final judgment.

6. No intent to avoid the judgment or mislead the court:
I want to be very clear that I am not seeking to avoid the judgment or change the responsible party. I fully understand that I am the defendant in this case and that the judgment is against me. I am already making monthly payments to Sunrise Medical Group, Inc. through their collection department. My only goal is to ensure that the court’s records accurately reflect my true legal name so that there is no confusion about my identity, either now or in the future.

7. Practical issues caused by the error (personal examples):
   • In September 2023, when I tried to set up automatic payments for this judgment through my bank, the bank’s customer service representative noted that the name on the judgment did not exactly match the name on my account. As a result, they initially refused to label the payment as "Judgment – Sunrise Medical Group, Maria E. Rodriguez" in their system and instead left it as a generic transfer, which made my personal budgeting and record-keeping more difficult.
   • In November 2023, when I applied for a small personal loan to help with moving expenses, the lender’s third-party verification service reported back that there was a "possible fraud alert" because of the discrepancy between "Rodriquez" and "Rodriguez." I had to spend several hours on the phone and fax copies of my identification and the judgment to prove that I was the same person. This caused the loan approval to be delayed by nearly two weeks.
   • I am a single mother of a 9-year-old son, and I rely on credit and stable employment to provide for him. Any delay or confusion caused by this name discrepancy has a direct impact on my ability to manage our finances and maintain stable housing.

8. Requested amendment:
I respectfully request that the court issue an order amending the judgment to replace all occurrences of "Maria E. Rodriquez" or "Maria Rodriquez" with "Maria Elena Rodriguez, also known as Maria E. Rodriguez." If the court prefers, it would also be acceptable to list only "Maria Elena Rodriguez" as that is my full legal name as shown on my birth certificate and driver’s license.

9. Good faith and accuracy:
I am making this request in good faith and solely for the purpose of correcting a clerical spelling error so that the court’s judgment accurately identifies me. To the best of my knowledge and belief, all the information I have provided in this declaration is true and correct. If the court requires any additional documentation (such as certified copies of my identification or proof of address), I am willing to provide those promptly.

I declare under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the foregoing is true and correct.
